title: File upload vs text input vs single-question page vs multi-step form
summary: Use file upload when the user must attach a document or media file; use text input for typed values, a single-question page to decide whether evidence is needed, and a multi-step form when upload is one guarded step in a longer journey.
decisionRules: Prefer file upload when the service needs the original document, image, scan, certificate, spreadsheet, or media file rather than a typed summary of its contents.
Prefer text input when the user can provide the needed value directly, such as a reference number from the document, without forcing them to find and attach a file.
Prefer a single-question page before upload when the first decision is whether the user has evidence, what kind it is, or whether the upload route should appear at all.
Prefer a multi-step form when file upload depends on earlier eligibility, identity, case type, or review answers and must preserve progress across pages.
Use a standard file upload before a drag-and-drop uploader when the main requirement is reliable file selection, clear labels, selected filename display, and accessible keyboard operation.
Reserve drag-and-drop upload for workflows where users commonly move files from a desktop folder and the same area still provides a button or link that opens the file chooser.
Reserve bulk import for structured records that need parsing, row validation, mapping, partial success handling, and import reports rather than a normal evidence attachment.
State accepted file types, maximum size, file count, and whether upload happens immediately or only after the surrounding form is submitted.
Show selected filename, upload progress, uploaded state, remove control, file-specific errors, and retry path before users continue.
Validate selected files on the server as well as the client because accept filters and filename extensions do not prove safe or valid content.
failureModes: A service asks for a file when a typed reference number would satisfy the decision and be easier for mobile users.
The upload label says Attach evidence but the hint never states accepted formats, maximum size, or whether photos are allowed.
A selected file disappears after an error, forcing the user to browse for the same document again.
The page says uploaded when the file is only selected locally and the final form submission has not happened.
Drag-and-drop is the only path, so keyboard, mobile, and assistive technology users cannot add a file.
A spreadsheet import is treated like a simple file attachment, so row errors and partial imports have no recovery path.

sources.note: GOV.UK supports labelled file upload, hints, errors, multiple selection, count-limit messaging, and improved upload behavior.
Carbon supports button/drop-zone wording, selected filename display, loading, success, uploaded, invalid states, removal, keyboard activation, and focus return.
MDN supports native file input behavior, accept filters, multiple selection, FileList access, and validation caveats.
DWP research supports setting expectations, upload control, timely feedback, mobile edge cases, and clear upload versus submit distinction.
